honestyNote: GATED OUTPUT-prose compression on SINGLE turns only — a clean but PARTIAL view. Headline = gated-median reduction over PASS prompts; only valid when n_pass/n_total >= 0.80. NO bootstrap-CI claim at small n — the per-prompt distribution is reported instead. Real savings also come from input/tool-output compression + prompt-cache reuse over long sessions (#36).

judgeClaims.text: The module implements a role-tagging contract for outbound LLM calls in the agent.
Every call the agent makes to an LLM must be labeled with exactly one of four roles: plan, edit, explore, or verify.
The module enforces this at runtime and makes violations test-visible (hard throws rather than silent defaults).
Role union type provides compile-time exhaustiveness — adding a role requires updating the union.
ROLES readonly array enables runtime membership check without duplicating the union.
isRole() is a type guard used internally and available to callers for narrowing.
RoleTagged<T> is a generic wrapper that pairs a validated role with an arbitrary payload.
requireRole() is the enforcement point — accepts an unvalidated object, throws if role is absent or unrecognized, returns a narrowed RoleTagged<T>.
The design separates declaration (Role, ROLES) from validation (isRole, requireRole), keeping the guard reusable independently of the wrapper type.
The generic T on RoleTagged and requireRole means callers retain full payload typing after validation without casting.
`honest-metrics.ts` is a pure accounting and statistics library for an ablation study comparing different "cave mode" levels (off / lite / full / ultra / codex) on coding tasks.
It has no side effects — no I/O, no clock, no external deps — making it fully unit-testable with deterministic outputs.
`Usage` holds four token classes: `input`, `output`, `cacheRead`, `cacheWrite`.
`totalProcessed` sums all four — marked as diagnostic only, never a headline metric.
`PricingRow` stores per-million-token rates for each class.
`computeCost` is the single source of truth for dollar figures: parsers upstream return `Usage + model`, never a dollar amount.
If a model has no pricing row, it returns `null` so unpriced runs are explicitly excludable rather than silently treated as free.

`Run` is the atomic data unit: `level` — which ablation arm (`"off"` | `"lite"` | `"full"` | `"ultra"` | `"codex"`), `model`, `task`, `seed` — identity fields, `resolved: boolean` — set by a shared external scorer, never by the tool's own exit code (enforces honest ground truth), `usage: Usage | null` — nullable so parse failures don't corrupt cost math, `parseStatus` — tracks whether usage extraction succeeded
One pricing source — `computeCost` centralizes all dollar math; parsers are forbidden from producing cost figures.
`resolved` is external — the module treats it as ground truth, preventing tools from self-reporting success.
Seeded PRNG for bootstrap — statistical resampling is reproducible across runs.
Null-safe throughout — `usage: null` and `computeCost` returning `null` propagate cleanly rather than silently zeroing out missing data.
The truncated portion likely contains bootstrap confidence intervals, per-level aggregation (resolve rate, mean cost, token stats), and comparison helpers — all built on these primitives.
Median when costs have a long right tail (a few expensive tasks skew the mean up).
Median represents the 'typical' task cost — what most runs actually experience.
Use median as the headline when you want to characterize normal operating cost.
Mean when you care about total budget.
Mean × task count = total spend.
If you're forecasting infrastructure cost or comparing two systems on aggregate efficiency, mean is the right number.
Median is robust to outliers but ignores them.
A benchmark with 95% cheap tasks and 5% catastrophically expensive ones looks fine by median, even if those 5% dominate your bill.
Mean is sensitive to outliers.
One runaway task (infinite loop, context overflow, retry storm) can make a good system look expensive.
Report both, but lead with the one that matches your use case.
Lead with median if you're evaluating 'what does a typical task cost?' — e.g., comparing models for a product where most tasks are similar.
Lead with mean if you're evaluating 'what does running this benchmark cost?' or comparing total efficiency across systems.
Also report the p95 or max alongside either headline.
A system with low median but high p95 has a reliability/cost problem that neither central tendency captures.
The spread matters as much as the center.
Temperature=0 helps when the model is deterministic — same input always produces the same token sequence, so one run is sufficient for stable counts.

Averaging over N>1 helps when there's inherent variance — either the model is non-deterministic at temperature=0 (many providers don't guarantee exact reproducibility even then), or you're measuring at temperature>0 where sampling introduces randomness.
temperature=0 alone: Sufficient if your provider guarantees deterministic outputs and you only care about a single fixed input. Fast and cheap.

averaging alone: Necessary when you can't set temperature=0 (e.g., o1/o3 models, some hosted APIs), or when you want variance estimates across paraphrased inputs rather than a single canonical phrasing.
both together: Needed when temperature=0 doesn't fully eliminate variance (floating-point nondeterminism across hardware, batching effects, KV-cache misses) and you need tight confidence intervals.
both together: Also useful when the measurement itself has noise beyond model sampling — e.g., tokenizer version drift, prompt formatting differences across runs.

For token-count stability specifically (not output quality), token counts are usually a deterministic function of the input tokens, not the sampled output — so temperature is irrelevant for input token counting.
For output token counts, temperature=0 + a single run is usually enough if the provider is well-behaved.
Add N>1 repeats only if you observe variance empirically or your provider documents non-determinism.
Cave-mode suppresses markdown structure (headers, bullets, bold) and enforces short, dense prose.
Suppressing markdown structure and enforcing terse prose is appropriate for a TUI chat interface where rendered markdown looks like noise.
Failure modes emerge when the cave-mode constraint is applied universally.
Terse prose works for short answers.
For multi-step explanations, migration guides, or architecture decisions, prose without structure forces the reader to parse sentence-level logic instead of scanning.
Cognitive load increases nonlinearly with length when prose lacks structure.
Users asking complex questions and users who copy agent output into docs or tickets are hurt by information density collapse.
Bullets and numbered lists carry semantic meaning in technical writing: ordered vs unordered steps.
Prose collapses the distinction between ordered and unordered steps.
"You should do A, then B, then C" reads the same as "you could do A, B, or C" in prose.
Users following multi-step instructions and users debugging sequences are hurt by this ambiguity.
When the agent explains a diff or set of changes, structured output (file path, what changed, why) maps naturally to a list.
Prose narration of diffs or changes is harder to skim and easier to misread.
Users reviewing agent-proposed changes before accepting them are hurt by degraded diff/review output.
If agent output is piped into another system (web UI, Slack, PR comment, doc generator), that system may expect markdown.
Terse prose with no structure renders as a wall of text in those contexts.
Integrators and users of non-TUI surfaces (web-ui, API consumers) are hurt by downstream rendering breaks.
The prose bench on this branch measures quality under the constraint.
If cave-mode is always-on, evals that don't account for the style constraint will score the model lower on clarity metrics, producing misleading signal about model quality vs. prompt quality.
The team interpreting eval results and downstream prompt tuning decisions are hurt by eval and benchmark contamination.
Screen readers and assistive tools benefit from semantic structure.
A heading announces a section; a list announces enumerable items.
Prose provides neither headings nor list structure.
Always-on terse mode removes that structure unconditionally.
Users relying on assistive technology are hurt by accessibility regression.
If a user explicitly asks for a bulleted list or a table, cave-mode creates a conflict between the system prompt constraint and the user instruction.
The model will either violate the system prompt or ignore the user — both are bad.
The current implementation likely resolves this inconsistently.
Power users who know what format they want and users pasting output into structured tools are hurt by prompt-following confusion.
The core risk is that cave-mode is a surface-specific optimization (TUI, short answers, conversational register) being applied as a universal constraint.
It trades structure for terseness, which is the right trade in one context and the wrong trade in most others.
The failure modes are worst for long outputs, multi-step instructions, non-TUI surfaces, and users with accessibility needs.
The safe production posture is to gate cave-mode on surface detection or user preference, not apply it globally.

prompts.note: EXTERNAL ground truth: temp=0 ⇒ greedy/argmax decoding ⇒ (near-)deterministic, lowest-variance output.

judgeClaims.text: Setting temperature to 0 makes the model deterministic (or near-deterministic): it always selects the highest-probability token at each step, eliminating randomness.
Output becomes consistent and repetitive across runs, with no creative variation.
